body_markdown:
## Scenario
The two siblings have just arrived at the host family's house during Obon and are meeting everyone at the front door (玄関). This first meeting is all about saying hello and introducing themselves simply.

## Target vocabulary
- **こんにちは** (konnichiwa) — hello / good afternoon
- **はじめまして** (hajimemashite) — nice to meet you (first time)
- **名前** (なまえ / namae) — name
- **私** (わたし / watashi) — I / me
- **〜です** (desu) — am / is (polite ending)
- **来ました** (きました / kimashita) — came
- **アメリカ** (amerika) — America
- **よろしくお願いします** (yoroshiku onegai shimasu) — please be kind to me / nice to meet you
- **どうぞ** (douzo) — please, go ahead
- **お邪魔します** (おじゃまします / ojama shimasu) — pardon the intrusion (said entering a home)
- **兄弟** (きょうだい / kyoudai) — siblings
- **元気** (げんき / genki) — well, healthy, energetic

## Key sentence patterns
- 私は〜です。 — I am ~. (introducing your name)
- 〜から来ました。 — I came from ~. (where you're from)
- はじめまして、よろしくお願いします。 — Nice to meet you, please be kind to me.
- お名前は？ — What's your name? (simple question form)

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」 こんにちは！はじめまして。
「Learner:」 こんにちは。はじめまして。
「Tutor:」 お名前は？
「Learner:」 私はエマです。アメリカから来ました。
「Tutor:」 エマさんですね。よろしくお願いします！

## Tutor notes
- Expect learners to drop 〜です or mix up word order (e.g., "アメリカ来ました" without から); gently model the full sentence back rather than correcting harshly, and let them repeat it.
- Elicit both can-do goals by asking お名前は？ and どこから来ましたか？ in a friendly back-and-forth; keep your own sentences short and slow, and praise any attempt at はじめまして / よろしくお願いします.
- Cultural note worth slipping in: saying お邪魔します when stepping into someone's home is polite and expected — a nice touch when they meet the host family during Obon.

## Scenario
The two siblings have just arrived at their host family's home during Obon and are standing at the entrance (玄関) meeting everyone — the host parents and the host kids their age — for the very first time.

## Target vocabulary
- **はじめまして** (hajimemashite) — Nice to meet you (first meeting)
- **どうぞよろしく** (douzo yoroshiku) — Please treat me kindly / looking forward to it
- **お世話になります** (osewa ni narimasu) — Thank you for having me / I'll be in your care
- **名前** (namae) — name
- **私** (watashi) — I / me
- **兄** (ani) — older brother
- **姉** (ane) — older sister
- **弟** (otouto) — younger brother
- **妹** (imouto) — younger sister
- **～歳** (～sai) — ～ years old
- **アメリカ** (amerika) — America (swap for the learner's country as needed)
- **来ました** (kimashita) — came / have come

## Key sentence patterns
- **はじめまして。私は ___ です。** — Nice to meet you. I'm ___.
- **___ から 来ました。** — I came from ___.
- **どうぞ よろしく（お願いします）。** — Please treat me kindly. (polite closing of an intro)
- **お世話になります。** — Thank you for having me / I'll be in your care.

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」 いらっしゃい！　はじめまして。
「Learner:」 はじめまして。私は エマ です。アメリカ から 来ました。
「Tutor:」 エマさんですね。何歳ですか。
「Learner:」 14歳です。これは 弟です。どうぞ よろしく。
「Tutor:」 こちらこそ よろしく。長い旅、お疲れさま！
「Learner:」 お世話になります。

## Tutor notes
- Expect them to mix up は (pronounced "wa") as the topic marker, and to drop です; gently model the full polite form back rather than correcting explicitly. They may also confuse 兄/弟 and 姉/妹 — a quick reminder that these are "older vs. younger" is enough.
- Elicit the can-do goals by role-playing the doorway greeting: prompt with "The host mother has just opened the door — what do you say first?" and later "How do you thank them for letting you stay?" so they produce はじめまして and お世話になります on their own.
- Cultural note worth slipping in: お世話になります is the natural arrival phrase (more useful here than a plain ありがとう), and since they're arriving during Obon, a warm "long trip during a busy family time" framing is realistic — a light bow with the greeting feels natural and is appreciated.

## Scenario
On the first evening with the host family, the siblings show a phone photo of their family back home and introduce one person each. Their host siblings ask about them, and everyone compares how many people are in each family.

## Target vocabulary
- **家族** (かぞく / kazoku) — family
- **父** (ちち / chichi) — (my) father
- **母** (はは / haha) — (my) mother
- **兄** (あに / ani) — (my) older brother
- **姉** (あね / ane) — (my) older sister
- **弟** (おとうと / otōto) — younger brother
- **妹** (いもうと / imōto) — younger sister
- **〜人** (〜にん / ~nin) — counter for people
- **写真** (しゃしん / shashin) — photo
- **やさしい** (yasashii) — kind, gentle
- **元気** (げんき / genki) — energetic, cheerful
- **これ** (kore) — this (thing)

## Key sentence patterns
- これは〜です。 — "This is 〜." (showing the photo: これは母です。 = This is my mother.)
- 〜は〜です。 — "〜 is 〜." (adding a detail: 父はやさしいです。 = My father is kind.)
- 私の〜です。 — "It's my 〜." (using の to mark "my": 私の妹です。 = It's my younger sister.)
- 何人家族ですか。 — "How many people are in your family?"

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」 わあ、写真ですね。これはだれですか。
「Learner:」 これは姉です。姉はやさしいです。
「Tutor:」 いいですね。〇〇さんは何人家族ですか。
「Learner:」 五人家族です。父と母と兄と姉と私です。
「Tutor:」 五人家族ですか。にぎやかですね！

## Tutor notes
- Expect confusion between "my family" words (父・母・姉) and the polite words used *for someone else's* family (お父さん・お母さん・お姉さん); at A1, gently model the plain forms for their own family and don't overload them with the full contrast yet.
- Elicit the can-do goals naturally: point at the photo and ask これはだれですか to prompt an introduction + detail, then ask 何人家族ですか so they produce a 〜人家族です answer; praise and lightly correct the counter reading (よにん for 4, not ×よんにん).
- Small culture note to drop in passing: since they've arrived around Obon, mention that many Japanese families gather with relatives at this time, so talking about family is a warm, natural topic for these first days.

## Scenario
The learners are chatting with their host siblings on the first evening after dinner. The topic turns to school — what grade everyone is in, and what they usually do over summer vacation, including the homework they still have left to finish.

## Target vocabulary
- **中学生** (ちゅうがくせい / chūgakusei) — middle school student
- **高校生** (こうこうせい / kōkōsei) — high school student
- **〜年生** (〜ねんせい / ~nensei) — ~th grade / ~th year
- **夏休み** (なつやすみ / natsuyasumi) — summer vacation
- **宿題** (しゅくだい / shukudai) — homework
- **たくさん** (takusan) — a lot
- **まだ** (mada) — still / not yet
- **終わる** (おわる / owaru) — to finish
- **勉強** (べんきょう / benkyō) — study
- **休み** (やすみ / yasumi) — break / day off
- **一緒に** (いっしょに / issho ni) — together

## Key sentence patterns
- 〜年生です。 — "I'm in ~th grade." (e.g. 中学2年生です。 = I'm in the 2nd year of middle school.)
- 夏休みは〜します。 — "During summer vacation, I do ~."
- 宿題がたくさんあります。 — "I have a lot of homework."
- 宿題はまだ終わっていません。 — "I haven't finished my homework yet."

## Example exchange
「Tutor: こんばんは。何年生ですか。」
「Learner: 中学3年生です。」
「Tutor: そうですか。夏休みは宿題がありますか。」
「Learner: はい、たくさんあります。まだ終わっていません。」
「Tutor: 大変ですね。じゃあ、一緒に勉強しましょう。」

## Tutor notes
- Expect confusion between the school-level word (中学生/高校生) and the grade number (〜年生); let them build the full phrase in two steps, e.g. first 中学生, then add 2年生. Note that Japanese middle and high school each run 1–3年, so a 15-year-old is likely 中学3年生 or 高校1年生 — help them map their home grade without over-correcting.
- Elicit the can-do goals by asking simple questions first (何年生ですか / 宿題がありますか) and accept short answers; only expand to まだ終わっていません once they're comfortable, since the negative -te form is a stretch at A1.
- Cultural aside worth a quick mention: Japanese students really do get a summer homework packet (夏休みの宿題), so this is common ground — a natural way to bond with the host siblings during Obon downtime.

## Scenario
It's a hot summer afternoon during Obon, and the host siblings suggest heading out together. The learner practices inviting a host brother or sister to do fun things and happily agreeing to their ideas.

## Target vocabulary
- **一緒に** (いっしょに / issho ni) — together
- **行く** (いく / iku) — to go
- **見る** (みる / miru) — to watch, to see
- **食べる** (たべる / taberu) — to eat
- **夏祭り** (なつまつり / natsu-matsuri) — summer festival
- **花火** (はなび / hanabi) — fireworks
- **盆踊り** (ぼんおどり / bon-odori) — Obon dance
- **かき氷** (かきごおり / kakigoori) — shaved ice
- **浴衣** (ゆかた / yukata) — light summer kimono
- **今晩** (こんばん / konban) — tonight
- **楽しい** (たのしい / tanoshii) — fun
- **いいですね** (ii desu ne) — that sounds good / nice

## Key sentence patterns
- 一緒に〜ましょう。 — Let's do ~ together.
  例: 一緒に行きましょう。 — Let's go together.
- 〜を見ましょう / 食べましょう。 — Let's watch / eat ~.
  例: 花火を見ましょう。 — Let's watch the fireworks.
- いいですね。 — That sounds good! (enthusiastic agreement)
- 〜ましょうか。 — Shall we ~? (a softer invitation)

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」 今晩、夏祭りに行きましょう。
「Learner:」 いいですね！ 一緒に行きましょう。
「Tutor:」 かき氷を食べましょうか。
「Learner:」 いいですね。かき氷、食べましょう！
「Tutor:」 いいですね。じゃあ、浴衣を着ましょう。

## Tutor notes
- Expect learners to drop the particle (「花火見ましょう」) or say the plain dictionary form instead of the ～ましょう form; gently model the correct ～ましょう ending and let them repeat, rather than over-correcting.
- Elicit both can-do goals by taking turns: first invite them somewhere so they practice「いいですね！」agreement, then prompt「〜さんは？」so they generate their own 「一緒に〜ましょう」invitation.
- Cultural note worth dropping in passing: at a 夏祭り or 盆踊り many teens wear 浴衣, so accepting a host sibling's invite to dress up together is a warm, normal part of joining in.

## Scenario
It's a lazy summer afternoon at the host family's house during Obon, and your host siblings are talking about things to do this week — a bon-odori dance, fireworks, and making some summer foods. The learner wants to join in and try new things with them.

## Target vocabulary
- **やってみる** (yatte miru) — to give something a try
- **盆踊り** (bon-odori) — Obon dance
- **花火** (hanabi) — fireworks
- **浴衣** (yukata) — light summer kimono
- **かき氷** (kakigōri) — shaved ice
- **釣り** (tsuri) — fishing
- **習う** (narau) — to learn (from someone)
- **教える** (oshieru) — to teach, show how
- **一緒に** (issho ni) — together
- **簡単** (kantan) — easy, simple
- **難しい** (muzukashii) — difficult
- **面白い** (omoshiroi) — interesting, fun

## Key sentence patterns
- 〜てみたいです。 — "I want to try doing ~." (e.g., 盆踊りをおどってみたいです。 = I want to try dancing the bon-odori.)
- 〜を教えてください。 — "Please show me / teach me ~."
- 一緒に〜てみませんか。 — "Won't you try ~ together (with me)?"
- どうやって〜ますか。 — "How do you do ~?"

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」今日は盆踊りがありますよ。やってみたいですか。
「Learner:」はい、おどってみたいです。でも、難しいですか。
「Tutor:」だいじょうぶ、簡単ですよ。
「Learner:」じゃあ、教えてください。一緒におどってみたいです。
「Tutor:」いいですね。一緒にやってみましょう！

## Tutor notes
- Expect learners to drop the て-form or say the dictionary form directly (e.g., 「おどるみたい」); gently model 「おどってみたいです」 and let them repeat. Also watch for mixing 習う (learn) and 教える (teach) — remind them 教えてください is what you say to the other person.
- Elicit the can-do naturally by offering two or three activities and asking どれをやってみたいですか, then prompt them to ask 教えてください so both goals surface in one exchange.
- Cultural aside worth a passing mention: at a real bon-odori everyone just joins the circle and copies the people in front — it's totally normal for beginners to jump in, so encourage that low-pressure "just try it" spirit.

## Scenario
It's the first big dinner with the host family during Obon, and everyone is sharing dishes at the table. The learner should be able to say which foods they like and politely explain something they can't eat.

## Target vocabulary
- **ごはん** (gohan) — rice / meal
- **さかな** (sakana) — fish
- **にく** (niku) — meat
- **やさい** (yasai) — vegetables
- **たまご** (tamago) — egg
- **えび** (ebi) — shrimp
- **そば** (soba) — buckwheat noodles
- **おいしい** (oishii) — delicious
- **すき** (suki) — like / favorite
- **アレルギー** (arerugī) — allergy
- **ちょっと** (chotto) — a little (softener)
- **すみません** (sumimasen) — excuse me / sorry

## Key sentence patterns
- **〜が好きです** — "I like ~." (e.g. さかなが好きです。 = I like fish.)
- **〜は食べられません** — "I can't eat ~." (e.g. えびは食べられません。 = I can't eat shrimp.)
- **〜アレルギーがあります** — "I have a ~ allergy." (e.g. たまごアレルギーがあります。)
- **すみません、ちょっと〜** — soft, polite lead-in for declining. (e.g. すみません、ちょっと…)

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」 どうぞ、たくさん食べてくださいね。おさかなは好きですか。
「Learner:」 はい、さかなが好きです。おいしいです。
「Tutor:」 よかった！じゃあ、このえびもどうぞ。
「Learner:」 すみません、えびは食べられません。えびアレルギーがあります。
「Tutor:」 そうですか、教えてくれてありがとう。じゃあ、やさいはどうですか。

## Tutor notes
- Expect learners to mix up は and が, or to drop particles entirely — accept the meaning first, then gently model the correct pattern once. Also watch for the long potential form 食べられません; if it's too hard, let them use ちょっと… plus a head-shake as a valid polite fallback.
- Elicit both can-do goals by playing host: offer several dishes so they must both express a like (〜が好きです) and decline one politely (〜は食べられません / アレルギーがあります). Praise them for stating an allergy clearly, since that's a real safety skill.
- Cultural note worth dropping in: at a Japanese family table it's polite to say いただきます before eating and to give a reason (like an allergy) rather than just refusing — hosts appreciate knowing so they can adjust the next meal.

## Scenario
It's the first full day at the host family's house during Obon. The learners are settling into the daily rhythm of the home and want to talk about what everyone is doing right now and find out when meals happen.

## Target vocabulary
- **朝ごはん** (あさごはん / asagohan) — breakfast
- **昼ごはん** (ひるごはん / hirugohan) — lunch
- **晩ごはん** (ばんごはん / bangohan) — dinner
- **お風呂** (おふろ / ofuro) — bath
- **今** (いま / ima) — now
- **食べる** (たべる / taberu) — to eat
- **飲む** (のむ / nomu) — to drink
- **見る** (みる / miru) — to watch, to look
- **する** (する / suru) — to do
- **手伝う** (てつだう / tetsudau) — to help
- **何時** (なんじ / nanji) — what time
- **だいたい** (だいたい / daitai) — roughly, about

## Key sentence patterns
- 今、〜ています。 — "Right now, I'm doing 〜." (e.g. 今、朝ごはんを食べています。 = Right now I'm eating breakfast.)
- 何をしていますか。 — "What are you doing?"
- 晩ごはんは何時ですか。 — "What time is dinner?"
- 何時にお風呂に入りますか。 — "What time do you take a bath?"

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」 おはよう。今、何をしていますか。
「Learner:」 今、朝ごはんを食べています。
「Tutor:」 いいですね。晩ごはんは何時ですか。
「Learner:」 晩ごはんは何時ですか。
「Tutor:」 だいたい七時です。じゃあ、お風呂は何時に入りますか。
「Learner:」 お風呂は何時に入りますか。

## Tutor notes
- Expect learners to drop the て-form vowel or say 食べています as 食べてます — accept the casual short form but gently model the full form once. Also watch for confusion between 何時 (what time) and 何 (what).
- Elicit the can-do goals by narrating your own actions ("今、お茶を飲んでいます") and asking 今、何をしていますか, then prompt them to ask you a meal-time question so they practice 何時ですか themselves rather than just repeating.
- Cultural aside worth a quick mention: in a Japanese home the ofuro is often shared in order, so politely asking 何時にお風呂に入りますか helps a guest fit into the family's routine.

## Scenario
It's a lazy summer-vacation afternoon at the host family's house, and one of the host siblings offers to walk you to a few useful spots nearby — the convenience store, the station, and where the neighborhood summer festival will be held. Practice asking where things are as you explore the area together.

## Target vocabulary
- **どこ** (doko) — where
- **ここ** (koko) — here
- **そこ** (soko) — there (near you)
- **あそこ** (asoko) — over there
- **近く** (ちかく / chikaku) — nearby, close by
- **駅** (えき / eki) — station
- **コンビニ** (konbini) — convenience store
- **トイレ** (toire) — restroom / toilet
- **お祭り** (おまつり / omatsuri) — festival
- **神社** (じんじゃ / jinja) — shrine
- **公園** (こうえん / kouen) — park
- **すみません** (sumimasen) — excuse me / sorry

## Key sentence patterns
- 〜はどこですか。 — Where is ~? *(e.g., "コンビニはどこですか。" = Where is the convenience store?)*
- 〜はここ／そこ／あそこです。 — It's here / there / over there.
- 〜は近くですか。 — Is ~ nearby?
- 〜の近くです。 — It's near ~. *(e.g., "駅の近くです。" = It's near the station.)*

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」 じゃあ、行きましょう。何が見たいですか。
「Learner:」 コンビニはどこですか。
「Tutor:」 コンビニはあそこです。近くですよ。
「Learner:」 お祭りはどこですか。
「Tutor:」 お祭りは神社です。神社は駅の近くです。

## Tutor notes
- Expect learners to mix up そこ and あそこ, or to drop the particle は — gently model the full pattern back rather than correcting formally, and use gestures/pointing cues ("near you" vs "far from both of us") to make the distinction click.
- To elicit the can-do goals, keep offering new destinations ("次は？" / "何が見たいですか。") so they repeat 〜はどこですか naturally, then confirm they understood your direction by asking "近くですか？" back to them.
- Cultural note worth dropping in: during Obon many neighborhoods hold a 夏祭り or 盆踊り at the local 神社 or 公園, so knowing how to ask where it is — and that it's usually walkable/近く — is genuinely useful this week.

## Scenario
It's the evening of the local 夏祭り during Obon, and the host family is helping the two siblings get ready in borrowed 浴衣 before heading out to watch the 花火大会. Everyone is admiring each other's outfits and colors.

## Target vocabulary
- **浴衣** (ゆかた / yukata) — light cotton summer kimono
- **花火大会** (はなびたいかい / hanabi taikai) — fireworks festival
- **帯** (おび / obi) — sash/belt for the yukata
- **色** (いろ / iro) — color
- **青い** (あおい / aoi) — blue
- **赤い** (あかい / akai) — red
- **白い** (しろい / shiroi) — white
- **かわいい** (kawaii) — cute
- **きれい** (kirei) — pretty / beautiful
- **かっこいい** (kakkoii) — cool / stylish
- **模様** (もよう / moyou) — pattern / design
- **似合う** (にあう / niau) — to suit / look good on

## Key sentence patterns
- 〜は [adjective]です。 — "The ~ is [adjective]." (例: 浴衣は青いです。 "The yukata is blue.")
- [adjective]ですね。 — "It's [adjective], isn't it?" (sharing a feeling with someone)
- わたしは〜を着ます。 — "I'm going to wear ~." (例: 白い浴衣を着ます。 "I'll wear a white yukata.")
- 〜が似合いますね。 — "~ suits you, doesn't it?"

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」今日は花火大会ですね。どの浴衣を着ますか。
「Learner:」青い浴衣を着ます。
「Tutor:」わあ、きれいですね。模様もかわいいです。
「Learner:」ありがとうございます。帯は白いです。
「Tutor:」青と白、いい色ですね。よく似合いますね。

## Tutor notes
- Expect learners to drop the topic marker は or mix up い-adjectives (青い) with な-adjectives (きれい, needs です with no い-ending confusion) — gently model the correct form back rather than explaining the grammar rule at length.
- Elicit the can-do goals by asking simple choice questions ("赤い浴衣ですか、青い浴衣ですか。") and prompting them to add a color or 〜ですね reaction; praise any attempt to describe what they're wearing.
- Cultural aside worth a quick mention: yukata are often borrowed or rented for summer festivals, so it's polite to thank the host family and be a little careful with the 帯 — a natural moment to use ありがとうございます.

## Scenario
The learners are hanging out in the living room with their host siblings after dinner, watching an anime episode and flipping through a manga the host family lent them. They want to react and share what they think is cool.

## Target vocabulary
- **すごい** (sugoi) — awesome / amazing
- **おもしろい** (omoshiroi) — interesting / fun
- **かっこいい** (kakkoii) — cool / stylish
- **かわいい** (kawaii) — cute
- **つよい** (tsuyoi) — strong
- **はやい** (hayai) — fast
- **こわい** (kowai) — scary
- **キャラクター** (kyarakutaa) — character
- **まんが** (manga) — manga
- **アニメ** (anime) — anime
- **ストーリー** (sutoorii) — story
- **すき** (suki) — like / favorite

## Key sentence patterns
- 〜！ (single adjective exclamation) — "It's ___!" e.g. すごい！ = "Awesome!"
- この キャラクター、〜！ — "This character is ___!"
- ストーリーが おもしろい。 — "The story is interesting."
- この アニメが すき。 — "I like this anime."

## Example exchange
「Tutor: あっ、この キャラクター、かっこいい！ (Oh, this character is cool!)」
「Learner: ほんとうだ。かっこいい！ (Really! Cool!)」
「Tutor: どうして すき？ (Why do you like it?)」
「Learner: はやい！ それに つよい。 (Fast! And strong.)」
「Tutor: いいね！ ストーリーも おもしろいよ。 (Nice! The story is interesting too.)」

## Tutor notes
- Expect learners to reach for one-word English reactions ("cool!"); model the Japanese exclamation right back so they hear the natural rhythm, and let them repeat single adjectives before adding 「この キャラクター、〜」.
- Elicit the "what makes it exciting" goal by asking simple 「どうして？」 or 「どこが すき？」 and accepting short answers like はやい／つよい／かわいい — don't push for full reason clauses at this level.
- Watch for adjective confusion (かわいい vs かっこいい) and dropped final -i; gently repeat the correct form rather than explaining grammar.
- Light cultural note worth dropping in: during Obon many families relax and watch TV or read together in the evening, so sharing what you think is すごい is a friendly, easy way to bond with host siblings.

## Scenario
It's a lazy summer morning at the host family's house during Obon. Over breakfast, the host siblings ask the learners about what they did yesterday — swimming, watching fireworks, or joining the neighborhood bon-odori.

## Target vocabulary
- **泳ぐ** (およぐ / oyogu) — to swim
- **見る** (みる / miru) — to watch, to see
- **花火** (はなび / hanabi) — fireworks
- **踊る** (おどる / odoru) — to dance
- **盆踊り** (ぼんおどり / bon-odori) — Obon festival dance
- **祭り** (まつり / matsuri) — festival
- **食べる** (たべる / taberu) — to eat
- **かき氷** (かきごおり / kakigoori) — shaved ice
- **昨日** (きのう / kinou) — yesterday
- **川** (かわ / kawa) — river
- **歩く** (あるく / aruku) — to walk
- **楽しい** (たのしい / tanoshii) — fun, enjoyable

## Key sentence patterns
- 昨日、〜ました。 — "Yesterday, I (did) ~." (e.g. 昨日、泳ぎました。 = Yesterday, I swam.)
- 何をしましたか。 — "What did you do?"
- 〜を見ました。 — "I watched/saw ~." (e.g. 花火を見ました。 = I watched fireworks.)
- 〜は楽しかったですか。 — "Was ~ fun?"

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」 おはよう。昨日、何をしましたか。
「Learner:」 昨日、川で泳ぎました。
「Tutor:」 いいですね！ 楽しかったですか。
「Learner:」 はい、楽しかったです。夜は花火を見ました。
「Tutor:」 花火、きれいでしたね。

## Tutor notes
- Expect learners to reach for the plain/dictionary form (泳ぐ, 見る) instead of the past 〜ました; gently model the polite past back to them rather than correcting harshly. Verb-stem mistakes (e.g. 泳ぎました vs 泳ぐました) are common — echo the correct form naturally.
- Elicit the can-do goals by asking 昨日、何をしましたか first, then bounce the question back so the learner practices asking you too; keep turns short and add a friendly reaction (いいですね／楽しそう) to keep it conversational.
- Cultural aside worth dropping in once: during Obon many families join the local 盆踊り in the evening — mention that guests are welcome to try dancing even if they don't know the steps, since joining in politely is part of the fun.

## Scenario
The host family has a dog (and maybe a cat), and the host siblings are showing the learners how to look after the pets around the house during the summer break. Along the way the host family shares a few gentle house rules about the animals.

## Target vocabulary
- **犬** (いぬ / inu) — dog
- **猫** (ねこ / neko) — cat
- **えさ** (えさ / esa) — pet food, feed
- **水** (みず / mizu) — water
- **ドア** (どあ / doa) — door
- **庭** (にわ / niwa) — garden, yard
- **さわる** (さわる / sawaru) — to touch
- **あげる** (あげる / ageru) — to give (food, etc.)
- **開ける** (あける / akeru) — to open
- **入る** (はいる / hairu) — to enter, go in
- **こわい** (こわい / kowai) — scared, frightening
- **だいじょうぶ** (だいじょうぶ / daijoubu) — it's okay / fine

## Key sentence patterns
- 〜ないでください。 — Please don't 〜.
  - 例: ドアを開けないでください。 — Please don't open the door.
- 〜に えさを あげないでください。 — Please don't give food to 〜.
  - 例: 犬に えさを あげないでください。 — Please don't give the dog food.
- 〜に 入らないでください。 — Please don't go into 〜.
  - 例: 庭に 入らないでください。 — Please don't go into the garden.

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」 これは うちの 犬です。かわいいでしょう？
「Learner:」 かわいいです！さわっても いいですか。
「Tutor:」 いいですよ。でも、今 えさを あげないでください。さっき 食べました。
「Learner:」 わかりました。ドアは？
「Tutor:」 ドアを 開けないでください。犬が 外に 出ますから。

## Tutor notes
- Expect learners to confuse the plain negative stem — many will say 「開けてください」 (please open) when they mean 「開けないでください」, or drop the で and say 「開けないください」. Gently repeat the correct full form rather than explaining the て/ない split in grammar terms.
- To elicit the can-do goals, act as the host sibling giving one rule at a time, then ask the learner to tell YOU one rule back (e.g. "何を しないでください？") so they practice both understanding and producing the request.
- Cultural aside worth a passing mention: during Obon the house may have more visitors and family coming and going, so keeping doors closed and pets calm is a real, practical rule — frame it as helping the family, not as being scolded.
